description:
You are working as a junior web-developer on a legacy static site that lives in the directory  
   /home/user/my_website/public  

The public directory already contains three HTML files:

1. index.html  
2. about.html  
3. contact.html  

Your lead developer wants a tiny automation that will later be plugged into a larger CI/CD pipeline. They have asked you to:

1. Write a short Bourne-shell script called build_logs.sh and save it to  
      /home/user/scripts/build_logs.sh  

2. The script must:
   a. Create the directory /home/user/build_logs if it does not exist.  
   b. Count how many “*.html” files are currently present inside /home/user/my_website/public.  
   c. Produce (overwrite, not append) a log file named /home/user/build_logs/summary.log in the exact format described below.

3. summary.log must have **exactly** the following structure so that automated checks can rely on precise line numbers and values:

   Line 1  →  HTML_FILE_COUNT=<number_of_html_files>  
   Line 2+ →  Alphabetically-sorted list of the *.html filenames, one filename per subsequent line, no leading path, no extra whitespace.

   Example (values will differ if the file set changes):
   HTML_FILE_COUNT=42  
   about.html  
   index.html  
   …

   Notes:  
   • There must be no blank lines anywhere in the file.  
   • A final trailing newline after the last filename *is* required (normal POSIX text file).

4. Make the script executable so it can be invoked directly.

5. After writing the script, run it once to generate /home/user/build_logs/summary.log.

Deliverables the grader will check:

• /home/user/scripts/build_logs.sh exists, is executable, and contains straightforward Bourne-shell code (2–3 commands is enough).  
• /home/user/build_logs/summary.log exists and perfectly matches the format above. The numerical count on line 1 must equal the number of *.html files detected, and the list below must be alphabetically sorted and complete.

Create whatever intermediate directories you need, but do not move or rename any existing project files. No sudo access is available. If you need to verify anything, feel free to display console output, but remember that only the existence and content of the two files listed above will be graded.
truth:
Initial filesystem state supplied to the agent:

Directories:
- /home/user/my_website
- /home/user/my_website/public

Files already present inside /home/user/my_website/public:
1. /home/user/my_website/public/index.html   (arbitrary contents, irrelevant to grading)
2. /home/user/my_website/public/about.html   (arbitrary contents, irrelevant to grading)
3. /home/user/my_website/public/contact.html (arbitrary contents, irrelevant to grading)

Nothing else exists under /home/user/scripts or /home/user/build_logs at start.

Expected final state after correct task completion:

1. /home/user/scripts/build_logs.sh  
   • File exists.  
   • Mode has user-execute bit set (e.g., 0755 or 0700).  
   • Shell interpreter line optional but acceptable (#!/bin/sh).  
   • Script body may be as small as:  
       mkdir -p /home/user/build_logs  
       (count=$(ls -1 /home/user/my_website/public/*.html | wc -l); … )  
     but grader will not check the exact commands, only the resulting artefacts.

2. /home/user/build_logs/summary.log  
   • File exists.  
   • Expected exact contents (including trailing newline):  
       HTML_FILE_COUNT=3\n  
       about.html\n  
       contact.html\n  
       index.html\n  
   • Line endings are Unix LF.

Automated test procedure:
a. Verify build_logs.sh exists and is executable: test -x /home/user/scripts/build_logs.sh  
b. Verify summary.log exists: test -f /home/user/build_logs/summary.log  
c. Read summary.log; ensure first line equals “HTML_FILE_COUNT=3”.  
d. Ensure remaining lines equal the three filenames listed above, in same order, nothing more, nothing less.  
e. Ensure total line count is 4.  
f. Optional: re-run the script; check summary.log is regenerated (timestamp change) but contents remain identical.

No hidden ports, services, or additional files are required.
